era_context: The Crisis of the Third Century continues. Probus, the reigning emperor, is assassinated this year, and Carus, a military officer, seizes power. Carus immediately appoints his two sons, Numerian and Carinus, as co-rulers, establishing a family dynasty. Carus launches an ambitious military campaign against Sassanid Persia, achieving initial success. This is a period of attempted imperial recovery and military aggression after years of internal instability.

events.title: Carus proclaimed Roman Emperor; Probus assassinated
events.description: The Roman Emperor Probus is assassinated by mutinous soldiers at Sirmium. Carus, a military officer of Dalmatian origin, is proclaimed emperor by the army in his place. Carus immediately moves to consolidate power and appoints his two sons, Numerian and Carinus, as co-rulers. This marks a significant shift in imperial politics: Carus attempts to establish a family dynasty after years of rapid succession and civil war. Carus's reign is marked by military aggression, particularly against Sassanid Persia.

sources.name: Numismatic evidence (coins of Carus, Numerian, and Carinus)

events.certainty_note: Probus's assassination and Carus's accession are attested in multiple sources and confirmed by contemporary coinage. The date of 282 CE is secure.

events.title: Carus launches military campaign against Sassanid Persia
events.region: Mesopotamia and Sassanid frontier (modern Iraq/Iran border)
events.description: Shortly after his accession, Carus leads a major military campaign against Sassanid Persia. The campaign is initially highly successful, with Roman forces advancing into Mesopotamia, capturing the city of Ctesiphon (the Sassanid capital), and achieving significant territorial gains. This represents one of the most successful Roman offensives against Persia in decades. However, the campaign will be cut short by Carus's death in 283 CE, and the territorial gains will be largely abandoned by his successors.
events.certainty_note: The campaign's occurrence and initial success are attested in multiple sources. However, the extent of Roman success is debated—some sources exaggerate the capture of Ctesiphon. The campaign's details and duration are uncertain.

events.title: Numerian and Carinus appointed as co-emperors
events.description: Carus appoints his two sons, Numerian and Carinus, as co-emperors (Caesars or Augusti, depending on the source). Numerian is assigned to the East, where he will accompany his father on the Sassanid campaign. Carinus is assigned to the West, where he will govern and defend the Rhine frontier. This arrangement represents an attempt to establish a family dynasty and to divide imperial responsibilities. However, the arrangement will prove unstable, and both sons will face challenges within two years.

sources.name: Numismatic evidence (coins of Numerian and Carinus)

events.certainty_note: The appointment of Numerian and Carinus is confirmed by coinage and historical sources. The date of 282 CE is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The extent of Roman military success against Sassanid Persia is disputed. Some sources claim the capture of Ctesiphon; others are more cautious. The Sassanid perspective on these events is absent from surviving sources. The reasons for Probus's assassination are debated—some sources emphasise military discontent, others suggest broader political factors. The stability and effectiveness of Carus's family dynasty arrangement is questioned by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 282 CE is moderate but dependent on late compilations. No contemporary narrative sources survive. Coinage and inscriptions provide independent verification. The sources are shaped by authors writing after Constantine's rise and may bias their portrayal of this period. The Sassanid perspective is entirely absent. Military and administrative details are sparse. The sources may exaggerate Roman military success to enhance the prestige of the empire.

graph_edges.note: Probus's assassination in 282 followed from his reign in 281
Carus's accession in 282 immediately led to the Sassanid campaign
Carus's accession and family dynasty arrangement in 282 led to his death and the succession of his sons in 283
